author: @MrBijanhejazi
text:
Thanks for your informative and inspiring videos. If possible, I would like to ask you and/or your audience a couple of questions. I don't expect you to know the answer to all of them :)
1- what is the maximum temperature these sheets can tolerate? 260 C?
2- what happens to them if put in direct contact with a corrosive environment?
3- which environments are especially corrosive to graphene sheets? is boiling water/steam one of them?
4- From Wikipedia I have read that flat graphene sheets are unstable with respect to scrolling. Are your sheets prone to cracking when bent into a cylinderical shape?
5- And finally, what is their heat-to-power efficiency compared to TEG's?
Best regards and good luck!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: your best answer is to experiment with this yourself as I haven't done any of what you are looking at so I have no idea

author: @directeeccks
text:
Thanks for the explanation on the mechanics with the ink.  Cheers!

If anyone is interested in thermal electrics and want to understand it more fully.  Here is a really good paper by PL Hagelstein from M.I.T.  that gives you a basic understanding thermal electric conversion and calculations to get efficiency/conversion rates with The Carnot Limit.  This paper helped me a lot.
http://www.lenr-canr.org/acrobat/Hagelsteinthermaltoe.pdf
Have fun.XD

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good posting mate thanks - i have already referred your post to  another member of the group

replies.author: @zenmanproject
replies.text: direct have you done any further work with graphene tegs? I am going to attempt to combine aluminum gic and phosphorus gic inks together. But if you have already attempted  it, I’d love to learn from you!

author: @trailkeeper
text: I'm thinking the electrons need to go/travel from a high potential to a low potential once released from the atoms due to the heat.   Using similar metals as the conductor terminals, I'm not sure how the electrons would decide to go in one direction or the other easilly/efficienty.  However, you do mentioned a "directiionality" to the flow of electrons..  If the same conductors are used, perhaps the metal should go flat against the heat source to enhance the flow of electrons/holes.  Just an idea.﻿

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hiya mate - direkcteecks posted a link further down in the comments page to a good description of this type of thermoelectric generator. Different metals aren't needed and they shouldn't be placed against the heat source or it just won't work - have a look at the paper mate

replies.author: @trailkeeper
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Well, due to a difference in heat is needed, to have a difference in energy, and hence voltaic potential energy, only one metal should go against the heat source, that's what I mean't in the comment.  Putting some atoms of metal in the graphite matrix might be something to experiment with. I don't have much equipment.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
trailkeeper
certainly worth the experiment mate

author: @bernardstokes6550
text:
aha now your cooking on gas....
instead of plastic I would like to use thin ceramic wafers and use my wood burner as my hot side and the cooler side would be the water on its way to the heat exchanger in the fire. I aim to use compacted Boron nitride powder as the insulated thermal contact on both faces.
I have just bought a house and will be building my own wood burner and associated systems and have for some time wanted to try this idea out....
as they say watch this space.......
thanks for the update....

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Now that is good thinking - do you plan on doing a video - I would like to see that
